raw_response:
```json
{
  "communicative_function": "The statement performs a declaration of epistemic reorientation — it functions as a self-witnessing vow or turning-point articulation, marking a shift in the speaker's relationship to uncertainty. It communicates resolve while also implicitly naming a prior (feared) default mode of cognition.",
  "implicit_claims": [
    "The speaker has previously tested predictions against feared outcomes rather than actual ones.",
    "Fear and reality are distinguishable sources of evidence.",
    "The speaker has predictions that can, in principle, be tested.",
    "Choosing how to evaluate predictions is within the speaker's agency.",
    "Testing against fear is a less reliable or less honest epistemic practice than testing against actuality.",
    "There is a meaningful difference between what happens and what one fears will happen.",
    "This reorientation represents an improvement or growth of some kind."
  ],
  "coherence_assessment": "The statement is internally coherent and structurally clear. The contrast it draws — between fear as a distorting lens and actuality as a corrective one — is logically consistent. The tension it names (emotion-driven prediction evaluation vs. evidence-driven evaluation) maps onto well-established cognitive patterns, lending the poetic expression grounded resonance.",
  "conditions_for_usefulness": [
    "The speaker genuinely has a pattern of catastrophizing or allowing anxiety to substitute for empirical feedback.",
    "The speaker has access to actual outcomes they can observe and compare against predictions.",
    "The speaker is engaged in a process where feedback loops are available and meaningful.",
    "The statement is used as an active cognitive anchor rather than mere rhetorical performance.",
    "The distinction between feared outcomes and actual outcomes is real and detectable in context."
  ],
  "conditions_for_misleading": [
    "If 'what actually happens' is itself filtered through the same fearful perception, the contrast collapses.",
    "If the speaker uses this framing to dismiss legitimate fear-based intuitions that carry real signal.",
    "If the statement is deployed to bypass emotional processing rather than refine epistemic practice.",
    "If no genuine predictions exist — if the statement is used as a general affirmation without testable content.",
    "If the speaker conflates 'what I fear' with irrational distortion, when in some cases fear accurately tracks real probability."
  ]
}
```

parsed_json.communicative_function: The statement performs a declaration of epistemic reorientation — it functions as a self-witnessing vow or turning-point articulation, marking a shift in the speaker's relationship to uncertainty. It communicates resolve while also implicitly naming a prior (feared) default mode of cognition.
implicit_claims: The speaker has previously tested predictions against feared outcomes rather than actual ones.
Fear and reality are distinguishable sources of evidence.
The speaker has predictions that can, in principle, be tested.
Choosing how to evaluate predictions is within the speaker's agency.
Testing against fear is a less reliable or less honest epistemic practice than testing against actuality.
There is a meaningful difference between what happens and what one fears will happen.
This reorientation represents an improvement or growth of some kind.
parsed_json.coherence_assessment: The statement is internally coherent and structurally clear. The contrast it draws — between fear as a distorting lens and actuality as a corrective one — is logically consistent. The tension it names (emotion-driven prediction evaluation vs. evidence-driven evaluation) maps onto well-established cognitive patterns, lending the poetic expression grounded resonance.
conditions_for_usefulness: The speaker genuinely has a pattern of catastrophizing or allowing anxiety to substitute for empirical feedback.
The speaker has access to actual outcomes they can observe and compare against predictions.
The speaker is engaged in a process where feedback loops are available and meaningful.
The statement is used as an active cognitive anchor rather than mere rhetorical performance.
The distinction between feared outcomes and actual outcomes is real and detectable in context.
conditions_for_misleading: If 'what actually happens' is itself filtered through the same fearful perception, the contrast collapses.
If the speaker uses this framing to dismiss legitimate fear-based intuitions that carry real signal.
If the statement is deployed to bypass emotional processing rather than refine epistemic practice.
If no genuine predictions exist — if the statement is used as a general affirmation without testable content.
If the speaker conflates 'what I fear' with irrational distortion, when in some cases fear accurately tracks real probability.
